questions.q: Why must we pass to the Grothendieck group rather than just take isomorphism classes of vector bundles?
choices: Vector bundles do not form a set
$\mathrm{Vect}_\mathbb{C}(X)$ is a monoid under $\oplus$ but cancellation can fail
questions.hint: Even on $S^2$, $E\oplus\mathbf{1}\cong F\oplus\mathbf{1}$ does not force $E\cong F$.
questions.explain: $(\mathrm{Vect}_\mathbb{C}(X),\oplus)$ is an abelian monoid; the universal map to a group adjoins formal inverses, identifying $[E]=[F]$ exactly when $E\oplus G\cong F\oplus G$ for some $G$ (stable equivalence).

questions.hint: Vector bundles on a point are just vector spaces, classified by dimension.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Vect}_\mathbb{C}(\mathrm{pt})\cong\mathbb{N}$ via dimension, whose Grothendieck group is $\mathbb{Z}$. The rank map $K^0(X)\to K^0(\mathrm{pt})=\mathbb{Z}$ is the augmentation.

questions.q: Which structure on $K^0(X)$ comes from the tensor product of bundles?
choices: A commutative ring structure with unit $[\mathbf{1}]$
questions.hint: $\otimes$ distributes over $\oplus$ and the trivial line bundle is a unit.
questions.explain: $\oplus$ gives the addition, $\otimes$ gives the multiplication, and the trivial line bundle is the multiplicative unit. Pullback $f^*$ is a ring map, making $K^0$ a contravariant functor to commutative rings.

k-grothendieck-group.title: Grothendieck group $K_0$ of an exact category

questions.q: In Quillen's $K_0(\mathcal{C})$ of an exact category, what relation does each short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ impose?
questions.hint: Think of an exact sequence as splitting $B$ into $A$ and $C$ at the level of classes.
questions.explain: $K_0(\mathcal{C})$ is the free abelian group on isomorphism classes modulo $[B]-[A]-[C]$ for every short exact sequence; it linearises additivity through extensions.

questions.q: What is $K_0(\mathbb{Z})$, the Grothendieck group of finitely generated projective $\mathbb{Z}$-modules?
questions.hint: Over a PID every finitely generated projective is free, classified by rank.
questions.explain: Projectives over a PID are free, so the rank map is an iso $K_0(\mathbb{Z})\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathbb{Z}$. More generally $K_0(\mathcal{O}_F)=\mathbb{Z}\oplus\mathrm{Cl}(F)$ for a Dedekind domain.

questions.q: Why does $K_0(\mathrm{Vect}(X))$ recover $K^0(X)$ even though the definition references all short exact sequences?
choices: Vector bundles have no nontrivial extensions over compact $X$
Every short exact sequence of vector bundles splits, so the relation reduces to $[E\oplus F]=[E]+[F]$
questions.hint: Choose a Hermitian metric; the orthogonal complement provides a splitting.
questions.explain: On a paracompact base, any short exact sequence of vector bundles splits (pick a metric and take the orthogonal complement of the sub-bundle). So the only relation surviving in $K_0$ is direct-sum additivity, matching the naive Grothendieck group of the monoid.

questions.q: Bott periodicity says complex topological K-theory satisfies which isomorphism?
questions.hint: The Bott class lives on $S^2$, signalling the period.
questions.explain: Complex K-theory is $2$-periodic: $K^n(X)\cong K^{n+2}(X)$ via external product with the Bott class $\beta\in\widetilde K^0(S^2)$. Real K-theory $KO$ is $8$-periodic — the genuine surprise.

questions.q: What is the Bott class $\beta\in\widetilde K^0(S^2)$?
questions.hint: It is the reduced class of the Hopf line bundle on $\mathbb{CP}^1=S^2$.
questions.explain: $\beta=[\mathcal{O}(1)]-[\mathbf 1]\in\widetilde K^0(S^2)$ generates a copy of $\mathbb{Z}$. Multiplication by $\beta$ is the Bott periodicity isomorphism $\widetilde K^0(X)\xrightarrow{\sim}\widetilde K^0(\Sigma^2 X)$.

questions.q: Bott's original (1959) theorem on the homotopy groups of $U=\varinjlim U(n)$ states which periodicity?
questions.hint: $\pi_n(U)=\mathbb{Z}$ for $n$ odd and $0$ for $n$ even (and positive).
questions.explain: Bott proved $\Omega U\simeq\mathbb{Z}\times BU$, hence $\pi_n(U)\cong\pi_{n-1}(\mathbb{Z}\times BU)$, giving the $2$-periodic pattern $\dots,0,\mathbb{Z},0,\mathbb{Z},\dots$ — the homotopical shadow of the Bott class on $S^2$.

questions.q: On formal Chern roots $x_i$ of a bundle $E$, the Chern character is given by which formula?
questions.hint: It must be additive on $\oplus$ and multiplicative on $\otimes$ — only the exponential does both.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{ch}(E)=\sum_i e^{x_i}=\mathrm{rk}(E)+c_1(E)+\tfrac{1}{2}(c_1^2-2c_2)+\cdots$. The first option is the total Chern class; the last is the Todd class.

questions.q: Why does $\mathrm{ch}\colon K^0(X)\otimes\mathbb{Q}\to H^{\text{even}}(X;\mathbb{Q})$ become an isomorphism for finite CW $X$?
choices: Because it intertwines the Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence which collapses rationally
questions.hint: Rationally any cohomology theory is determined by its coefficients.
questions.explain: The Atiyah–Hirzebruch spectral sequence $H^p(X;K^q(\mathrm{pt}))\Rightarrow K^{p+q}(X)$ collapses after $\otimes\mathbb{Q}$, and $\mathrm{ch}$ realises the resulting iso. So K-theory and cohomology agree rationally; integrally K-theory carries finer torsion.

questions.q: How does the Chern character behave on tensor products?
questions.hint: Tensor adds Chern roots: $(E\otimes F)$ has roots $x_i+y_j$.
questions.explain: Since $e^{x_i+y_j}=e^{x_i}e^{y_j}$, $\mathrm{ch}(E\otimes F)=(\sum e^{x_i})(\sum e^{y_j})=\mathrm{ch}(E)\smile\mathrm{ch}(F)$. Combined with additivity on $\oplus$, this makes $\mathrm{ch}$ a ring map.

questions.q: For a commutative ring $R$, what is $K_1(R)$ as defined by Quillen's $+$-construction $K_n(R)=\pi_n(BGL(R)^+)$?
questions.hint: $\pi_1$ of $BG^+$ is the abelianisation of $G$, and the elementary subgroup $E(R)$ is the commutator subgroup of $GL_\infty(R)$ (Whitehead's lemma).
questions.explain: By Whitehead's lemma the commutator subgroup of $GL_\infty(R)$ is $E(R)$, so $K_1(R)=GL_\infty(R)^{\mathrm{ab}}=GL_\infty(R)/E(R)$. Over a field this is $\det\colon GL_\infty\twoheadrightarrow F^\times$; over a commutative local ring the determinant gives $K_1(R)\cong R^\times$.

choices: The kernel $\mathrm{St}(R)\to E(R)$ where $\mathrm{St}(R)$ is the Steinberg group
questions.hint: $\mathrm{St}(R)$ is the universal central extension of $E(R)$.
questions.explain: $K_2(R)=\ker(\mathrm{St}(R)\twoheadrightarrow E(R))$, the centre of the Steinberg group. Matsumoto's theorem identifies $K_2$ of a field with $F^\times\otimes F^\times/\langle a\otimes(1-a)\rangle$, the Milnor symbols.

questions.q: Why must Quillen apply the $+$-construction to $BGL_\infty(R)$ rather than just take its homotopy groups?
choices: $\pi_1(BGL_\infty(R))=GL_\infty(R)$ is non-abelian; the $+$-construction abelianises $\pi_1$ without changing homology, producing a space with the right $\pi_n$
The classifying space is too small
questions.explain: $BG$ is a $K(G,1)$, so its homotopy is concentrated in $\pi_1=G$. The plus-construction kills the perfect commutator subgroup $E(R)\subset GL_\infty(R)$ in $\pi_1$, leaving the abelianisation, while preserving homology — and creates higher homotopy. The result $\pi_n(BGL(R)^+)$ is Quillen's $K_n(R)$.

questions.q: In the Atiyah–Singer index theorem, the analytic index $\ind(D)=\dim\ker D-\dim\mathrm{coker}\,D$ is computed as which topological pairing?
questions.hint: Symbol $\to$ K-class on $T^*M$, then push to a number via Chern character and the Todd genus.
questions.explain: Atiyah–Singer: $\ind(D)=\langle\mathrm{ch}([\sigma(D)])\smile\mathrm{Td}(TM\otimes\mathbb{C}),[T^*M]\rangle$. The symbol class $[\sigma(D)]\in K^0(T^*M)$ encodes $D$ topologically; Chern character converts to rational cohomology and the Todd class corrects for the integrality.

questions.q: What is the rank of $K_n(\mathcal{O}_F)$ for a number field $F$ with $r_1$ real and $r_2$ complex places, in Borel's calculation?
choices: $r_1+r_2$ for $n=4k+1$ and $r_2$ for $n=4k+3$ ($k\ge 1$); zero in even positive degrees
questions.hint: The ranks of higher K-groups of $\mathcal{O}_F$ are governed by Dirichlet's unit-theorem pattern, alternating between $r_1+r_2$ and $r_2$.
questions.explain: Borel: $K_{4k+1}(\mathcal{O}_F)\otimes\mathbb{Q}$ has rank $r_1+r_2$ and $K_{4k+3}(\mathcal{O}_F)\otimes\mathbb{Q}$ has rank $r_2$ for $k\ge 1$, while even-degree higher K-groups have torsion only. $K_1(\mathcal{O}_F)=\mathcal{O}_F^\times$ recovers Dirichlet's unit theorem (rank $r_1+r_2-1$, plus roots of unity).

questions.q: Quillen's calculation of $K_n(\mathbb{F}_q)$ gives which answer for $n\ge 1$?
questions.hint: $K$-theory of a finite field is purely torsion in odd degrees and zero in positive even degrees.
questions.explain: Quillen (1972) computed $K_{2k-1}(\mathbb{F}_q)\cong\mathbb{Z}/(q^k-1)$ and $K_{2k}(\mathbb{F}_q)=0$ for $k\ge 1$, with $K_0(\mathbb{F}_q)=\mathbb{Z}$ and $K_1(\mathbb{F}_q)=\mathbb{F}_q^\times$. Borel's theorem combines this with localisation sequences to get $K_n(\mathcal{O}_F)$.
